summary: Anglo-Irish philosopher and politician, often considered the father of modern conservatism. Author of Reflections on the Revolution in France, A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful, and A Vindication of Natural Society. Argued for gradual change and tradition over abstract revolutionary principles. Led the impeachment trial of Warren Hastings. Influenced Joseph de Maistre and Michael Oakeshott. Attacked by Mary Wollstonecraft and Thomas Paine.

works.name: Reflections on the Revolution in France
works.description: Burke's most famous work (1790) was prompted by a sermon by Richard Price and argues that the French Revolution would end badly because it was based on abstract principles that did not account for society's complexities. Burke champions tradition, 'natural rights,' and 'prejudice' (educated tradition) over 'naked reason.' He includes fond personal recollections of Marie Antoinette. The work was translated into French by Louis XVI. It was attacked by Mary Wollstonecraft's A Vindication of the Rights of Men and Thomas Paine's Rights of Man. Burke's Letters on a Regicide Peace later urged Britain not to make peace with the Directory. Russell Kirk's The Conservative Mind (1954) discusses Burke first.

works.name: The Sublime and Beautiful
works.description: Burke's A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757) was the first work to treat the beautiful and the sublime as opposites. The sublime arises from terror, 'vastness' (greatness of dimension), and is connected to self-preservation. The beautiful is associated with 'delicacy' and causes 'love or some passion similar to it.' Burke identified pleasure, pain, and delight as three types of passions, and succession and unity as components of the 'artificial infinite.' The work was critiqued by Kant in his Observations on the Feeling of the Beautiful and Sublime and the Critique of Judgment. Burke also discussed blackness's aesthetic power as 'partial darkness.'

works.name: General / Biographical and Reception
works.description: Burke is commonly identified as the 'father of conservatism.' A Vindication of Natural Society (satirizing Lord Bolingbroke) is sometimes considered the first expression of philosophical anarchism despite being satirical. He led the prosecution in the impeachment trial of Warren Hastings. He influenced Joseph de Maistre (his 'French equivalent') and Michael Oakeshott (On Being Conservative, On Human Conduct). He had earlier supported the American colonists during the Revolution. Wentworth Woodhouse manuscripts revitalized Burke scholarship in the 1940s.

comprehensive_summary:
Edmund Burke (1729-1797) is a consistently clued figure bridging political philosophy and aesthetics. The two universal giveaways are Reflections on the Revolution in France and being called the 'father of conservatism.'

Reflections on the Revolution in France (1790) was prompted by Richard Price's sermon and argues the French Revolution was doomed because it relied on abstract principles rather than tradition. Burke champions 'prejudice' (educated tradition) over 'naked reason' and includes fond recollections of Marie Antoinette. Mary Wollstonecraft attacked it in A Vindication of the Rights of Men and Thomas Paine in Rights of Man. It was translated into French by Louis XVI. Russell Kirk's The Conservative Mind (1954) discusses Burke as the founding conservative thinker.

A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757) was the first to treat beauty and sublimity as opposites. The sublime arises from terror, 'vastness,' and self-preservation; the beautiful causes love and is associated with 'delicacy.' Kant's Observations and Critique of Judgment were inspired by Burke's distinction. Burke identified blackness's aesthetic power as 'partial darkness.'

A Vindication of Natural Society (satirizing Bolingbroke) is sometimes considered the first expression of philosophical anarchism, though it was intended as satire. Burke led the impeachment of Warren Hastings and had supported the American colonists. He influenced Joseph de Maistre and Michael Oakeshott. He is also occasionally confused in tossups with literary theorist Kenneth Burke (dramatistic pentad).
